speaker: interviewer
text: --16, 1994. The survivor is Helen Beck, and this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, you were telling me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about the encounter with the Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Could you tell me more detail, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was on our way back home to Krakow, and we stopped somewheres to find lodging overnight. You know, we were a group of people going together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 14 people coming from the Schindler factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 18 people, 14 men and 4 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia towards Krakow. When we arrived in one village, the men found somewheres like a-- a barn. And we thought we're going to spread the straw, and everything, and that's how we're going to spend the night. However, not far from this-- in the village, not far from the barn, there was a home, a group of Russian soldiers. And somehow they came over there, and they took one of the girls-- no, it was not me-- and whatever he wanted to do. But the boys were so intervening so hard, they didn't permit-- I told him not going to talk about it. But it-- it shows you they had no mercy, even the Russian soldiers, because we told them, and they could see we did not look, you know, perfectly well physically from the camps. So we told him that we are survivors, we have come from the camps. We are trying to reach our home. But he took one of the girls, but nothing happened to her because the boys ran somewhere, they got the high officer, the Russian officer. He came and everybody, so he released the girl. But this is besides the point, I didn't like to mention about this experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in prison camp uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing what I was wearing, it was not me because I was very [? bashful. ?] For my sister and some other girls, they went out to the village in [INAUDIBLE], and they brought some clothing for me. They brought me a beautiful pair of shoes, which I didn't know what it means how to wear shoes. Everything was fine. The only thing, that the shoes were one size too small. And that's I walked. It was not easy for us, the survivors. Yet, I would like to mention how difficult it was with us to start life. In spite of everything, we tried very hard. We were so determined, even like I said-- and I am not ashamed to repeat-- that the world did not care what happened to us. And nobody-- there was no Red Cross, nobody came forward. As I already said, without repeating, the best thing-- what they did, they put us into DP camps. And when we finally came-- and maybe I should say it because my husband mentioned on his own-- when the time came and the opportunity came that we could immigrate to the United States when President Truman signed the DP quota. There was always a however or a but. Yes, we could register. Yes, we could come to the United States. However, we need somebody who will guarantee when we come to this country, we will not become a burden to the-- to the country. So the only thing, again, in our life, the Jewish people, the organizations play a major role. So there was, at that time, the Jewish-- the Joint signed an affidavit-- affidavit, and some kind of place of working, all kinds of papers. They were our sponsors. When we came to the United States, we arrived in Lynn, Massachusetts with a 10-month-old baby. I was not feeling too great physically. There were many problems starting to roll-- to roll up and fro, and everything is a direct result from being in the concentration camps. But we were always grateful for the smallest things. I could not believe because I saw how the organizations worked. And I felt that some day, whenever I will be physically capable, I will undertake certain things with the organization, and become involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the organizations do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Lynn, number one, they secured for us a place, a roof over our head. We-- when we arrived, of course, for the first few nights, they put us up in a hotel, which was great. But this was expensive. They found an elder couple, and they secured a room with this older couple. So the room was, size-wise, very, very small. Between the bed and the clip, there were very few steps, three. It was so crowded, but we had the privileged kitchen and privileged bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for me, I felt like the Queen of-- of England. I am in my own home, and I don't have to be in a camp. You know, life was different. The feeling, the respect for ourselves came to life again.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were over there a few months only, yet the Jewish community found a place. It was a difficult. And they came, they told us if you would like to have an apartment on your own, we could secure it for you. But we are telling you now, the place is a pigsty. It's dirty. It's-- it stinks. It smells. If you can undertake cleaning up, painting, whatever, it's gonna be your responsibility. Without hesitation, we took the apartment. My husband was working very hard because not only he worked on his job for $0.75, wherever he found somebody, and he worked on a second job. Saturdays, half-day Sundays in order to get the extra few dollars in order to support his family. We did get the apartment. We cleaned it up. And people wouldn't-- couldn't believe what we made from this apartment, how we cleaned up. And they were amazed. And some guy who owned some property, many buildings, and when he came to visit us, out of curiosity, he says, gee, in your place, you can eat from the floor. And it didn't take long, and he said you do not belong in this section.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, another few months passed, a year or whatever, and he-- we-- he made it possible that we move to a very nice residential section in Lynn, Massachusetts. Again, with this condition that we going to clean this apartment. There was a French lady living in this apartment. She had maybe 20 cats in her apartment. So the heath-- the Board of Health from the city forced her to move out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We cleaned up. We lived in the nice section so-- because at that time, our son started to go to kindergarten. So he went to the better section in the city, a better school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the community help you in furnishing and-- and equipping the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They give us very, very old furniture, I never saw in my life. But you must appreciate one thing. We were grateful for the smallest things. And we could make-- you know, when they brought me torn curtains with holes. How I did it, I put together, I made from two one, from whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they came in and they couldn't recognize. Are those the curtains what we gave you? I'm just giving you examples. We never were sitting back, but we never also tried-- we never expected give me, give me. We wanted to achieve on our own. Did you join a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time was very difficult. You see, even at that time. Today, we know because we are involved and we know very well from zero ground how we are helping out the Russian people or other people in need. No, they did not give us a membership to their temples. But we saved a few dollars. We knew this is very important, so we pay for the tickets for the High Holy Days. Simple things like that. The worst thing was nobody can understand-- and I want you to listen-- you can be the best parent. You can do your best for everything in household and everything. You can only be a parent, but you cannot be a grandparent. You cannot do other things. Our children, because later on, four years later, we had a second son who was born in Lynn. Our children were deprived of the most precious things. Our children never knew what it means, a grandparent. I will give you an example. When we were already in the section, what I say was the better residential section. In the same building lived another Jewish family. They happened to have a little girl, she was only maybe four months older than our son. But she was a very frail child, quiet, and our son was a boy. All boy, running, doing things, and he was very inquisitive child. This little girl who lived above us, her grandmother lived only maybe two or three blocks. Two blocks because she walked to it. And it was a habit we learned, and she was the first and only grandchild. She would come religiously every day, bringing an ice cream for Susan mid-afternoon. Since Susan loved our boy because they got along so well, by being friendly with our boy, she learned how to eat. She learned how to walk. She was willing even to play. Otherwise, she was sitting like a China doll in her apartment. So they were getting along very nicely, the two children. And this was late in the afternoon. My husband was working. He would come from work like 4 o'clock, 4:30. Between 4:00 and 4:30.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mid-afternoon, and the father of this little girl, he used to work in Boston. And he would come home from work sometimes after 6:00. So they would have supper later. We would have supper earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing, what it was, so when he used to see Susan every day having ice cream brought by her grandmother, but she was very good. Sometimes she'd give him a lick of ice cream. They were both happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, he-- while I'm in the kitchen preparing dinner, he ran in from outside, red like a flame, screaming. I couldn't understand what he wants, what he's screaming. And he came to me, and he pulled my skirt, almost trying to rip off and crying how come Susan's grandmother brings her almost every day ice cream? Where's my grandmother with [INAUDIBLE]? It was difficult for us. I challenge you, what would you do? The only thing I did, I hugged him, and I hold onto him very closely.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he quiet down after a while, quiet. So I took his hand, and I told him Bobby, daddy's coming soon from work, we're going to have dinner. And after dinner, we're going out and having the biggest ice cream, any flavor you want. And after the ice cream, we're gonna take a ride by the ocean and you're going to be running around. He used to love to throw pebbles into the water. So I told him we're gonna do it so he quiet down. This was not the end of the hardship for the children of survivors because they grew up deprived from the most precious things. When our son, he was in middle school, and it happened. It was a blessing for us. They were very good students, and we tried our best. I assisted. My husband was working, I was always home for them. Whatever to fill in, that they should never come to an empty house. Our children seldom talked about our experience, and we did not feed them with the horrors what we lived through. When he was in the middle school, being a good student. And the children, both, especially the oldest one, in sports, debate teams. You name it, he was in it. They had an assignment from the teacher to do a family tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know. Maybe-- and I will admit, I probably didn't understand the curriculum how they teach here. My teaching from back home, it's different.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they have their assignment. So it went by one day, two days, three days. And the teacher said, Bob, you didn't turn in your assignment. What happened? I don't know how it was, what it was, how he tried to explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he came from the school, and it was a different day. Because he entered the house not even looking at me, he went directly to his room, closed the door, and I felt something was wrong. I came up only with one idea. I bet you boy are boys. He was playing with the kids. He got in a fight, somebody got hurt or whatever. This was my imagination that what happened because his behavior led me to believe something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a hour or two hours, I am knocking on the door, and I hear him like not wanting to talk. But I'm knocking, and I says, Bob, I want to talk to you. He let me in the room, and I was asking something went wrong, what happened? He burst into tears, and he told me. All the children of the whole class turned in the paper, and he says I did not know what to do. What can I write on my family tree? Because some students in the class would read their assignment how they would spending, you know, vacations, going fishing with grandparents, doing certain things. One-- or some boys would say I have the watch I got from my grandmother, grandfather, uncle for my bar mitzvah and everything. And here is a child, nothing. So we were talking, and I did not know what to say to him. But I-- I tried to take him out from the depression, from his anger. And I told him, the best thing is tomorrow morning when you go to the class, asked Ms. [? Skelly. ?] She was an excellent student-- teacher. Ask her that you want talk to her in private. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he revealed to her that he's a child of survivors, the teacher threw her hands in the air, not knowing that in her class there is somebody a little bit different than the rest of the students. She understood him very well. And he felt like a stone came up from his chest. He explained then she told him, Bob, you could write this and this, that's not necessary. But he could. She gave him-- she even embraced him, and he felt better. So this was the first time that they found out, and the class and the teachers that Bob Beck is a child of survivors. There were many other things how it was difficult for us to deal with life. Like I said before, and without repeating, but-- but there were other different, similar things. You can only be one thing. You can be a parent, you cannot be a grandparent, and all kinds of things. However, we were very fortunate that the children did very, very well in school. And they enjoyed the nicest friends they made. And through our children, our life was in enriched, because they made wonderful friends, and the parents of the children became our friends. Then it was, you know, like-- like a chain, one ledge to the other. And I remember when one lady-- very comfortable, very rich people. Professionals, doctors-- their son became like one with our boy. They loved each other, they had so much in common. So their parents became our friends. And this lady, no matter how rich she was, she was very much involved in all kinds of Jewish organizations. And she automatically, come join me doing this and do that. I followed because I wanted to become part of the community, and so we did. For my husband, it was easy. You know, he joined the men's club, whatever, B'nai B'rith. But I joined the women's organization. But little did I know, when you belong to an organization, you need money, you know, to make contributions. It was not easy. Money was very scarce. And another thing, I am a big believer that charity starts at home. And whatever money I could save up and I tried-- I did not deprive the children-- but I saved up as much-- every dollar, every penny-- and I was sending to Israel because the family in Israel were in real need. So to me, this was number one. But when you belong to an organization, you cannot. You must be part of it. I came up with an idea. If, you know-- I cannot go back, I am already involved in [? Hadassah ?] [? and ?] [? Sisterhood. ?] I'm involved in other organizations where we were trying to raise money for other people. So I didn't want to be on the market for pity, so I made a decision instead to have an extra blouse in the closet or have an extra pair of shoes. So instead, buying for myself, and I could have used it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this money went to make the contribution wherever I belong. And it didn't take long both of us, and the children saw, and they were also involved in the youth organizations. So it takes dedication in order to achieve. We were dedicated. Then again, like my husband said, we were members in a temple. Do us and give us by that time, and the children thrived on it because they were also of the same temple. Like I said, the youth organizations. And it was a-- a-- a meaningful life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said, later on in the 70s, when the first professors, the deniers came out that the Holocaust never happened again. And again, without repeating, we were terribly hurt. People didn't want to listen to this. But you could not sit and dwell on those things. You go forwards. And the rabbi was over there helpful because he was not only our rabbi, he became our teacher. He was our friend. He had children the same age with our children. And he was the one who did not sit quiet. He spoke up for the Jewish organization-- for the people in-- in-- in Poughkeepsie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi's name was-- he passed away a few years ago-- and-- Rabbi [? Zimmet, ?] and he was the one-- he would-- many times, when my husband was working, children were in school, and I hear the phone ringing. Here's Rabbi [? Zimmet. ?] Helen, I need you. You come because we are going. And I-- I was a little bit bashful. He would take me, and we would travel to different colleges, all mainly to interfaith churches, all kinds of organizations, and he would speak up how dare the people have the nerve to deny the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. At that time, I was afraid. The only thing, while sitting next to the rabbi, he would take me by the hand and he would speak to the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, do you want to show us some pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pictures? I don't have-- I would like only those two. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, she did. She does want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say first? This is my husband, Kuba Beck. We meet after the liberation in Krakow. We happened to be in some of the same camps. However, we never spoke. We never saw each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after the liberation in Krakow in 19-- middle of 1945, we met in Krakow. We start to go out, and we were married in 1946 in Bamberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to say? Should I say something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing I would say that, you know, we were very fortunate to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are very lucky--

speaker: interviewee
text: --working for Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and very fortunate that we did survive and we did something with our lives after the liberation. We brought up two young sons. And they are married. They are both professional. They have their children. For us to see our children and our grandchildren is the greatest triumph, the greatest joy because each time, when I look at the children, I see that the Nazis lost. They did not succeed what they want because their aim was not to have no survivors, no witnesses to those awful, terrible horrors what we witnessed. Our children are the justification, and I hope they are the link to our future. And we only hope they should succeed, and they should enjoy good health and be our future generations. From generation to generation to represent our people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my-- my-- my wife mentioned that we didn't really speak to our kids about our past experience. We didn't feed them too much. However, they know all about it. They do have all our tapes and all of our involvement. And I hope when we are gone that they will continue because we feel that this is very important subject to talk about. And hopefully, by speaking up, we will prevent from happening again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will only mention that two of our grandchildren saw the movie And one of our son-- the one who lives in Milwaukee-- he took his daughter. He was invited to a few schools to speak about about his parents who are survivors. We have one granddaughter in Westchester, the younger one, I can-- Alison-- Alison Beck. When they were here in February, we went to Miami to show them the Holocaust Memorial. And I could see how touched both of them were, but the younger one even more. And before we entered into the car, coming back from Miami, she didn't ask, but she make a statement. Papa, Nana, you know when you come-- we are going to Israel, by the way, in April. And she make this statement, Papa, Nana, after you come from Israel, you are coming to speak to my class. So we have an agent in our family. Yes, we are very, very proud of our children and grandchildren. And I hope they gonna be always a good addition to our people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We are looking now at the picture, which was taken in November 1993, when we flew to Milwaukee for the bat mitzvah of our granddaughter, Stacy Beck. In this picture, they are our four grandchildren. Here is-- on the edge is Sarah Beck. Next is her mother, Rosie Beck. Next is Lorie Beck. And then is our precious granddaughter-- they're all precious-- this is Alison Beck. Then is Jeremy Beck. Stacy Beck, the bat mitzvah girl. Our son, Arthur. My husband, Kuba Beck and myself. This was a wonderful, wonderful date. On this picture, we can see our four grandchildren, our precious people. Sarah Beck and her sister, Alison Beck. Stacy Beck, the bat mitzvah girl. And her brother, Jeremy Beck. This was in Milwaukee, November 1993. On this picture is my husband, Kuba Beck, and myself. This picture was taken last year when they discovered that we are Schindler's survivors. And there were many newspapers. When they found out, they came to the house. This was one of the pictures for publicity reasons-- purposes. But this was last year, taken after the movie was shown.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be 1994?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is also in November, late November, December of 1994. This picture was taken right here in Del La Ray Beach. In this picture, there's a very unusual picture. After 49-- 50 years, we met Mrs. Schindler, Emily Schindler. She was invited here to Florida by the Simon Wiesenthal Organization. And she was honored in one of the temples here, B'nai Torah, in Boca Rotan. And for the first time, after this many years, we were with Emily Schindler. And it always will remain an unforgettable reunion.
